narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 46% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stems from a careful look at key aspects of your profile, like your undergraduate performance, class rankings, and family background. Among the factors that shaped this outcome, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most influential, actually working strongly in your favor by suggesting a solid foundation that pushes toward bar exam success, especially since your 3.90 is notably higher than the 3.24 average among those who passed. Next in influence was your Year 3 class decile ranking of 1, which pulled the prediction in the opposite direction toward failure risk, as it's much lower than the near-top deciles typical for bar passers. Your low family income bracket played a smaller but still positive role, nudging the odds slightly toward passing, even though it's less common among successful examinees where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate. Compared to students who were admitted—those with stronger Year 3 rankings around the 6th decile on average, balanced by high LSAT scores near 37—your lower LSAT of 26.5 and early-year decile of 1 amplified concerns despite your impressive undergrad record and full-time study plans. These elements together tipped the model's view toward caution, prioritizing bar passage as a core measure of fit for our program. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your strong GPA highlighting your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to typical admitted students' levels, as it weighs heavily in these predictions. Strengthening recent academic rankings through targeted coursework or extracurriculars could also help demonstrate upward momentum. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas, or exploring preparatory programs that have helped others in similar situations succeed on the bar. Your path isn't defined by this one prediction—many have turned things around with focused effort.
